title: AI Scenarios: From Doomsday Destruction to Do-Nothing Bots!
gist: Damodaran dissects the Citrini Research AI-doomsday scenario (unemployment above 10% and markets down ~40% by June 2028) and the Citadel rebuttal, then offers his own 'possible-plausible-probable' framework for judging AI disruption, concluding disruption will be real but slower and narrower than doomsayers claim, hitting software, banking and consulting unevenly.

themes.theme: The Citrini doomsday scenario and market fallout

insights.claim: A sell-off in markets was triggered at least partly by an AI scenario assessment from Citrini Research, a macro and stock research publisher, released on February 22, 2026.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Watch for research-driven, narrative-based sell-offs even absent new fundamental data — sentiment on AI disruption is now a standalone market catalyst.
insights.locator: a sell off last week, in response, at least partially, to an AI scenario assessment from Citrini Research

insights.claim: The Citrini report's dark scenario projects that by June 30, 2028, AI disruption will have pushed unemployment above 10% and driven the stock market down almost 40%, framed by Citrini as a 'scenario, not a prediction.'
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A concrete tail-risk date (mid-2028) and magnitude (10%+ unemployment, -40% market) gives investors a specific hedge-timing reference even if Damodaran doubts its probability.
insights.quote: unemployment rates rising above 10% and the market down almost 40% in response
insights.locator: The Citrini AI assessment came out on February 22, 2026

insights.claim: Unlike prior AI doomsday narratives that assume AI fails to deliver and the pain comes from wasted trillions of capex, the Citrini scenario assumes AI works very well and is adopted very quickly, and the pain comes precisely from that success displacing high-earning white-collar workers, cutting their consumption and triggering rising default risk, wider credit spreads, and a private-credit collapse.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Distinguish 'AI fails, capex wasted' bear cases from 'AI succeeds too well, labor market implodes' bear cases — they imply opposite trades (short AI capex names vs. short consumer discretionary/credit).
insights.locator: In contrast, the Citrini story is built on the expectation that not only does AI work well

insights.claim: Negative market reactions had already appeared around new AI investment announcements at Meta and Amazon, alongside markdowns in software company market capitalizations, before the Citrini-driven sell-off.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Capex-announcement reactions at Meta and Amazon are an early tell that the market is repricing AI infrastructure spend as a cost rather than a growth driver.
insights.locator: That disquiet has played out as negative market reactions to new AI investments at Meta and Amazon

insights.claim: Goldman Sachs, Moody's and JP Morgan responded to Citrini with more benign AI scenarios projecting modest and temporary job and income effects, while Citadel published a direct rebuttal arguing for a much more positive end game.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A cluster of major sell-side and buy-side shops rushing out competing AI-economic scenarios within days signals the topic has become a market-moving consensus battleground, not a niche debate.
insights.locator: I was surprised to see Citadel wade into the argument, with a direct rebuttal to Citrini

insights.claim: Citadel's rebuttal rests on three pillars: current job/layoff data in AI-exposed sectors like software show only modest losses with no breakout in adoption speed; history shows past disruptions (PCs, the internet) were never as fast or destructive as doomsday scenarios claim; and macroeconomic logic is inconsistent in assuming a large positive productivity shock produces a large negative shock to the economy and markets.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: These three tests (current labor data, historical disruption base rates, macro internal consistency) are a reusable checklist for stress-testing any future AI-doom scenario that circulates.
insights.locator: is built around three pillars

insights.claim: Block, the fintech company founded by Jack Dorsey, announced it would lay off almost 40% of its workforce and cited AI's rise as a factor, but Damodaran calls this a noisy signal since many tech companies have bloated workforces and use AI as convenient cover for corrections unrelated to actual AI-driven productivity gains.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't take AI-attributed layoff headlines at face value as proof of disruption speed — cross-check against headcount bloat and prior over-hiring before treating them as causal AI signals.
insights.quote: That is a noisy signal, though, since many tech companies have bloated work forces, and AI gives them easy cover, when correcting past mistakes.
insights.locator: The announcement by Block

insights.claim: Damodaran argues that nearly all AI scenarios in circulation (including Citrini's) are flawed because they describe a single detailed outcome without stating how likely it is, or list many scenarios without ranking their likelihoods, making the analysis nearly useless for decision-making.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Treat any AI 'scenario report' — including bank research — skeptically unless it assigns explicit or qualitative probability; unranked scenarios are effectively noise for position-sizing.
insights.quote: The problem with all of these AI scenarios is that they are rooted in the weakest of responses to uncertainty
insights.locator: The problem with all of these AI scenarios

insights.claim: Classic best case/base case/worst case scenario analysis is 'an almost useless exercise' because virtually no risky investment fails under its best case or survives its worst case, so it adds no discriminating information.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Discard best/base/worst-case decks as a decision tool; demand probability-weighted scenarios or full distributions instead.
insights.quote: an almost useless exercise, since there are almost no risky investments that are going to pass muster under the worst case scenario
insights.locator: In its most sloppy form

insights.claim: Damodaran proposes his '3P test' (used in company valuation) as the practical alternative for AI scenario analysis: classify a scenario as merely possible (it can happen), plausible (requires more backing), or probable (you can estimate a likelihood), since full probability-weighted scenario analysis is infeasible given how little is known about AI outcomes.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors can apply this same possible/plausible/probable filter to any AI-forecast they encounter to quickly discard low-value tail scenarios from actual portfolio decisions.
insights.locator: the 3P test that I use when valuing companies

insights.claim: Damodaran classifies Citrini's speedy, massive AI-disruption scenario and the opposite extreme (AI as a marginal, largely distracting productivity tool) as both merely 'possible but not quite plausible,' and states his own most probable scenario is that AI reduces workforces in a subset of businesses, produces some genuine game-changing tools, and takes longer to unfold and monetize than its advocates claim.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: His base case is: expect slower monetization timelines than AI bulls price in, but real (not zero) structural workforce reduction in specific sectors — a case for patience on AI capex payback rather than betting on imminent doom or imminent triumph.
insights.locator: As for probable, I think that disruption will reduce workforces in a subset of businesses

themes.theme: Disruption speed and magnitude — the real data

insights.claim: The Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis's AI adoption tracker shows that use of generative AI tools has grown faster in non-work settings than at work, suggesting personal devices are driving technology adoption ahead of institutional/workplace adoption.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Workplace AI productivity gains may lag consumer AI usage growth by a wide margin — don't extrapolate consumer engagement metrics into enterprise productivity or job-displacement timelines.
insights.locator: the use of Gen AI tools in non-work settings has grown more than its usage at work

insights.claim: Per the same Fed tracker, measured time savings from AI usage have been modest so far, ranging from under 1% in accommodation and food services to about 4% in information and management of companies.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Hard survey evidence undercuts both AI-doom and AI-euphoria narratives: actual measured productivity gains today are single-digit percentages, not the transformative step-change embedded in aggressive AI capex paybacks.
insights.quote: ranging from less than 1% in the accommodation and food businesses to about 4% in information and management of companies
insights.locator: Second, the time that AI has saved people

insights.claim: Damodaran concludes from the Fed survey data that AI usage today is 'neither as explosively fast growing nor as much of a time-saver' as its proponents claim, directly contradicting the adoption-speed assumption embedded in the Citrini doomsday scenario.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Use the Fed adoption tracker as an ongoing, checkable falsification test for the speedy-disruption thesis — if adoption/time-savings stay flat, the Citrini-style crash timeline (mid-2028) becomes less credible over time.
insights.quote: this graph suggests that AI usage is neither as explosively fast growing nor as much of a time-saver, as its proponents suggest that it is
insights.locator: Overall, this graph suggests

themes.theme: Four propositions on AI's economic aftershocks

insights.claim: Damodaran hypothesizes that disruption benefits are greater when AI directly displaces people than when it merely provides productivity tools, and that productivity effects shrink in economic value terms the longer the disruption takes to unfold.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Slow AI rollouts should be modeled with materially smaller productivity upside than headline capex or analyst projections assume — time erodes the economic value of the benefit.
insights.locator: Proposition 1

insights.claim: Damodaran hypothesizes that disruption costs are greater when AI displaces people (via lost income and spending power) than when it just makes existing workers more efficient, and that these costs also shrink the longer disruption takes, since delay gives new labor-market entrants time to adjust.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The scenarios with the largest combined stakes (biggest benefit AND biggest cost) are fast, mass-displacement scenarios; slow, tools-only scenarios are lowest-stakes in both directions — useful for scenario-weighting portfolio hedges.
insights.locator: Proposition 3

insights.claim: Damodaran's synthesis: if AI displaces workforces across many businesses quickly, the net economic effect is likely negative near-term because higher-earning white-collar workers are laid off simultaneously and the economy must absorb the shock fast; if displacement is limited in scale or slow to unfold, both benefits and costs shrink but the net effect is more likely positive in both the short and long term; and if AI is purely a productivity tool with little labor displacement, the disruption effectively 'fizzles' with minimal benefit or cost.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This is a decision matrix investors can map real-world data onto (layoff breadth x speed) to continuously update which quadrant the economy is actually tracking toward.
insights.locator: As you can see, the scenarios where AI displaces existing employees and happens quickly

themes.theme: Which jobs and industries are actually exposed

insights.claim: Software has been one of the first industries targeted by AI disruption because it is profitable, its products/services are logical and rule-based, and it carries little regulatory or systemic protection — but within software, Damodaran expects user-interface-heavy products to be more resilient than back-end/background software.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Differentiate within software holdings: back-end/infrastructure and rule-based software is more exposed to AI substitution than UI-heavy, human-interaction-dependent products.
insights.quote: it is profitable, its products and services are logical and rule-based and much of it has no regulatory or system protection
insights.locator: there is a good reason why software

insights.claim: Damodaran argues blanket claims about AI wiping out banking, consulting and law jobs are overwrought because a substantial portion of that work exists to satisfy legal/regulatory requirements or liability cover, not just to fill operating needs — giving those professions structural protection independent of whether AI could technically do the work better.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Legal/regulatory-driven professional services (fairness opinions, compliance-driven banking and legal work) are more AI-resistant than their task-complexity alone would suggest — a reason to be skeptical of aggressive AI-replaces-professionals valuation models for these sectors.
insights.locator: With banking and law, a substantial portion of the work done is to meet legal or regulatory requirements

insights.claim: M&A fairness opinions persist despite being largely useless in substance because they provide legal cover against lawsuits when deals fall apart; Damodaran doubts Delaware courts are ready to accept an AI-generated fairness opinion in place of a human banker's, even if the AI's analytical quality is better.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A concrete example of a job that survives AI disruption purely due to legal-liability function rather than analytical value-add — look for other 'liability theater' roles across finance and law with similar durability.
insights.quote: My guess is that the Delaware courts are not quite ready for an AI fairness opinion bot to take the stand and defend a deal
insights.locator: I have written about the inanity and uselessness of fairness opinions

insights.claim: Damodaran argues consulting fees are paid less for the advice itself and more so clients have 'someone else to blame' when things go wrong, meaning an AI consulting bot lacks the same liability-outsourcing value as an army of human consultants with elite MBAs.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Consulting firms' economic moat may be less about analytical output (which AI can replicate) and more about liability transfer — a distinct, harder-to-disrupt revenue driver not captured in simple task-automation models.
insights.quote: clients pay consulting fees not for the advice, but so that they have someone else to blame, when things go wrong
insights.locator: With consulting, where cookbook solutions are more the norm

insights.claim: Damodaran contends the true driver of white-collar AI vulnerability is not worker intelligence or training but the structure of the jobs themselves — many white-collar roles force bright, trained people into mechanical, rule-driven tasks rather than intuition- or principle-driven work, which is precisely what makes those tasks replicable by AI.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Assess AI-exposure of a role/company by how rule-driven vs. judgment-driven its actual day-to-day tasks are, not by the prestige or credentials of the people doing it.
insights.quote: it is the nature of the jobs that we have created in many white collar settings that makes them vulnerable to disruption, not the intelligence or training of the people holding those jobs
insights.locator: In short, it is the nature of the jobs

themes.theme: Cui bono — unequal distribution of AI's winners and losers

insights.claim: Damodaran argues the interests of AI companies and the rest of the economy/market structurally diverge: AI companies benefit most when disruption is broad and fast, while the rest of the economy benefits more from disruption that is slower and confined to fewer businesses.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Expect AI vendors/infrastructure players to keep promoting fast, broad-disruption narratives regardless of actual economic externalities — treat their public AI timelines as interest-conflicted, not neutral forecasts.
insights.quote: The interests of the AI companies and the rest the economy/market will diverge on AI disruption
insights.locator: The interests of the AI companies and the rest

insights.claim: Damodaran notes that even when a company's AI tools raise productivity and lower costs, this may not translate into higher profits for that company if competitors can access and adopt the same AI tools, competing away the gains.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Be cautious with bull cases that assume AI-driven cost savings flow straight to margins — if the tools are broadly available, competitive dynamics can arbitrage the savings away rather than letting them stick to any single company's bottom line.
insights.locator: This will be the case even if AI tools add to productivity

insights.claim: Damodaran draws a historical parallel: the internet, China's rise, and the smartphone disruption all tilted competitive dynamics toward larger companies, making industries more winner-take-all, and he expects AI disruption to play out similarly.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Favor scale incumbents over smaller challengers within AI-disrupted sectors, consistent with the historical pattern that major tech disruptions concentrate winnings among already-large players.
insights.quote: The last few major disruptions, starting with the internet, moving on the China and then the smartphone, have all tilted the playing field in many businesses towards larger companies
insights.locator: The last few major disruptions

insights.claim: Damodaran states it is not just plausible but likely that a strong AI disruption will worsen wealth and income inequality, with AI founders joining the ranks of deca-billionaires and centi-billionaires.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A structural, politically salient wealth-concentration outcome from AI is a plausible catalyst for future regulatory or tax intervention aimed at AI winners — a tail risk to price for mega-cap AI beneficiaries.
insights.quote: it is not just plausible, but also likely, that a strong AI disruption will make wealth and income inequality worse
insights.locator: At the individual level

insights.claim: Damodaran observes that because blue-collar workers who lost jobs to internet and China-driven disruption were largely ignored by society, there may be a degree of schadenfreude in how society responds to white-collar job losses from AI, even though a job is a source of self-esteem, not just income, and psychic damage to displaced professionals (engineers, bankers, consultants) will be significant.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The social/political reaction to white-collar AI job losses may be muted or even unsympathetic relative to the economic damage — a factor that could delay political/regulatory pushback against AI disruption compared to what pure economic modeling would predict.
insights.quote: there may be more than a hint of schadenfreude in society's response to white collar job losses
insights.locator: The fact that blue collar workers lost their jobs

themes.theme: Building a personal moat against AI displacement

insights.claim: Damodaran argues generalists have an edge over AI relative to specialists: AI can absorb the deep content that gives specialists their advantage, but multi-disciplinary thinking across unrelated fields is harder for AI to replicate.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Career and skills positioning takeaway: breadth across disciplines is a more durable differentiator against AI automation than depth within a single narrow specialty.
insights.locator: Generalist vs Specialists

insights.claim: Damodaran cites his own valuation philosophy of blending narrative/storytelling ('right brain') with financial modeling ('left brain') as harder for AI to replicate than pure numbers-driven analysis, though he admits the 'Damodaran Bot' AI trained on his material has visibly improved at storytelling over two years of observation.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Even self-styled 'hard to automate' skills like narrative/storytelling are being closed by AI faster than expected — professionals should not assume any single differentiator is permanently safe.
insights.locator: Left and Right Brain

insights.claim: Damodaran warns that as answers become instantly available via search and ChatGPT, people lose the need to exercise independent reasoning, weakening their 'reasoning muscles' and making themselves more vulnerable to being replaced by AI over time.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Habitual reliance on AI for answers may be self-defeating for long-term career resilience — deliberately preserving independent reasoning practice is a hedge against personal obsolescence.
insights.quote: we are losing the need to exercise those reasoning muscles, and exposing ourselves to being outsourced by our bots
insights.locator: Reasoning muscle

insights.claim: Damodaran uses Pascal's wager as justification for preparing against AI disruption even if one doubts it is imminent: since the expected value of preparing (acting as if an AI competitor is present) exceeds the expected cost of not preparing, the rational choice is to prepare regardless of the true probability of disruption.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A decision-theoretic justification for building AI-resilience skills/processes now as a low-cost hedge, independent of one's personal probability estimate for how disruptive AI turns out to be.
insights.quote: acting as if an AI presence and competitor is present will make you better at whatever you do, as a teacher, banker, consultant or software engineer, and that will persist, no matter what AI's impact is ultimately
insights.locator: I will draw on Pascal's wager

insights.claim: Damodaran discloses that an AI entity called the 'Damodaran Bot,' developed by his NYU colleague Vasant Dhar using his publicly available lifetime output (classes, books, writing, models, videos), has progressed over roughly two years to where it can replicate much of what he does almost effortlessly, despite occasional quirks and hiccups.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A live case study of how quickly a well-documented professional's expertise can be captured and reproduced by AI once sufficient public content exists — a cautionary signal for any expert with a large public content footprint.
insights.quote: I have watched that bot develop, with quirks and occasional hiccups, to a point where it can replicate much of what I do almost effortlessly
insights.locator: Just about two years ago, I wrote about an AI entity called the Damodaran Bot
